topic: Someone finds a wallet containing a large sum of cash
raw_output:
[story 1]

The leather of the wallet felt unnervingly heavy in my palm. It was thick, worn, and inside, a thick wad of bills spilled out, a startling cascade of green and brown paper. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did little to steady the sudden tremor in my hands. I stood frozen by the curb, the afternoon sun suddenly too bright, the sounds of traffic receding into a dull hum. I looked around, half-expecting someone to materialize, someone to point and demand an explanation. My mind raced, a frantic internal monologue trying to categorize the impossible situation, but all I could manage was a slow, deliberate blink, trying to force my gaze back to the mundane sidewalk, as if reality itself might be about to dissolve.

[story 2]

I tucked the find deep into my coat pocket, the weight of it a cold, hard anchor against my thigh. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming a silent alarm. My fingers kept twitching, wanting to smooth the edges of the unfamiliar currency, wanting to confirm that this wasn't a trick, a bizarre setup. I walked quickly, my pace uneven, my shoulders hunched as if bracing for an impact that wasn't there. The streetlights cast long, distorted shadows that danced with my anxiety. When I finally reached the relative safety of my apartment, I didn't bother with the door; I just stumbled inside, dropping the wallet onto the worn rug with a soft thud, and leaned against the wall, gripping the edge of the frame until my knuckles turned white.

[story 3]

It was just a wallet, I told myself, a misplaced item. But the sheer volume of money inside created a physical pressure behind my eyes. I kept glancing down at the contents, the numbers blurring into meaningless streaks of ink. A bead of sweat traced a path down my temple. I tried to rationalize it—a prank, a mistake—but the silence in the small park felt loaded, charged with an unspoken significance. I stood there for a long time, watching passersby, noting their casual movements, feeling intensely exposed under the scrutiny of the open air. When I finally moved, it was a jerky motion, as if my limbs were operating on borrowed energy, and I hurried away, desperate to put distance between myself and the object in my hand.
